nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, swamps, flooded zones, and small slow-moving rivers; bottom-dwelling demersal species that favours shallow, heavily vegetated or debris-strewn areas
nativeRange.biotope: Congo Basin / West African lowland swamp

decorPreferences: soft sandy substrate for resting and foraging
caves, driftwood, and rock overhangs as daytime shelters
dim or subdued lighting to encourage daytime activity
dense background planting or floating plants to reduce light

diet.notes: Strictly carnivorous; typically refuses dried foods. Prefers meaty live or frozen offerings: prawns, earthworms, mussel, lancefish, and similar. Hunts primarily by smell due to poor eyesight; target-feed with tongs or a feeding stick if kept with tankmates to ensure it receives food. Some individuals may eventually accept high-protein pellets or frozen bloodworm with patient conditioning.

goodWith: other Polypterus species of similar size
medium to large characins (e.g. African tetras)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole
small tetras, rasboras, and nano fish
slow-moving or long-finned fish vulnerable to ambush
very large aggressive cichlids that may harass it

summary: A smaller Congo Basin bichir reaching 30–35 cm, the Retropinnis Bichir is a nocturnal, air-breathing predator belonging to the lower-jaw clade of Polypterus. Hardy and relatively tractable with similarly sized tankmates, but a dedicated escape artist requiring a sealed lid.
careNotes:
Polypterus retropinnis is one of the smaller members of the genus, making it more manageable than giants like P. bichir, yet it still demands a spacious tank — a footprint of at least 120 cm × 45 cm (200 L) is recommended to allow adequate movement and territory. The species is demersal and spends most of its time on the bottom; floor space is more important than water depth. A soft sandy substrate (3+ cm) combined with caves, driftwood, and rock overhangs provides the sheltered retreats this nocturnal hunter requires during daylight hours.

Bichirs possess a divided, lung-like swim bladder and must access the water surface to breathe atmospheric air — a tightly fitting, weighted, or lockable lid is non-negotiable, as P. retropinnis is a powerful and persistent escape artist capable of squeezing through surprisingly small gaps. Keep the water level 5–10 cm below the top rim and tape or weight any gaps around cables and filter pipes.

Water quality should be maintained with regular partial water changes; while the species tolerates a wide pH (6.0–8.0) and moderate hardness, accumulated nitrates above ~40 ppm are stressful over time. Temperature of 25–28°C is optimal. P. retropinnis has poor vision and hunts by smell; it should be fed at night or in dim light to reflect its natural foraging rhythm. Use tongs or a feeding stick to place food directly in front of the fish when housing it with other species. Frozen prawns, earthworms, mussel, and lancefish are ideal staples; most wild-caught individuals resist dried foods, though patience and gradual mixing can sometimes convert them to accept high-protein pellets.

In a community context, P. retropinnis is broadly peaceful toward fish too large to swallow — robust catfish, similarly sized bichirs, medium characins, and larger knife fish are generally compatible. Anything small enough to fit in its mouth will eventually disappear. Multiple bichirs can cohabit but should be of comparable size; dominant individuals may monopolise food. See also: [P. palmas] (formerly synonymised with West African retropinnis reports), [P. senegalus] (Senegal bichir, smaller beginner-friendly relative).
breedingNotes: Not yet recorded in captivity for this species. Based on the breeding biology of related Polypterus species, spawning is presumed to involve egg-scattering in dense vegetation; slightly soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.8) and a gradual temperature rise mimicking seasonal rainfall are likely triggers. Eggs reportedly hatch in approximately 3–4 days and larvae become free-swimming after a further 2–3 days, at which point they accept brine shrimp nauplii or microworms. External gills are visible in newly hatched larvae and are reabsorbed as the juveniles develop their labyrinthine air-breathing apparatus.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus retropinnis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (35 cm SL), temperature (25–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–25°H), minimum tank dimensions (120 cm × 45 cm, 200 L), full native range across 11 countries, habitat (marshes, swamps, flooded areas, small rivers), diet (meaty live/frozen), compatible and incompatible tankmates, escape-risk warning (tight-fitting lid essential), and preliminary breeding notes (not yet bred in captivity; presumed egg-scatterer).

additional.name: FishBase — Polypterus retropinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 34.0 cm SL, males/unsexed), distribution (Ogowe and Congo River basins, Central Africa), habitat (small rivers, swamps, flooded zones; freshwater demersal), taxonomy (Order Polypteriformes, Family Polypteridae), diet (carnivore, trophic level 2.6 ± 0.26), facultative air-breathing trait, IUCN Least Concern (2019). FishBase max length (34.0 cm SL) aligns closely with SeriouslyFish (35 cm); 35 cm used as the practical aquarium maximum.

additional.name: FishBase — DRC Country Checklist (freshwater)
additional.notes: Confirmed presence of Polypterus retropinnis in the Democratic Republic of Congo freshwater fauna checklist, corroborating Congo Basin distribution cited in the species brief.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet recorded in captivity. Presumed egg-scatterer based on congeners; likely requires soft slightly acidic water and temperature conditioning to trigger spawning. Larvae hatch in ~3–4 days; fry free-swimming ~3 days later and accept brine shrimp nauplii.
